mūla.devanāgarī: अभ्यास-योग-युक्तेन चेतसा नान्य-गामिना | परमं पुरुषं दिव्यं याति पार्थानुचिन्तयन्

mūla.speaker: Krishna

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If the destination depends on the final moment of consciousness at death (as BG 8.6 insists), what happens to years of practice when a deathbed distraction arises — is the entire sādhana negated, or is habit structurally protective?
Rāmānuja distinguishes abhyāsa (informal daily rehearsal) from yoga (formal timed upāsanā): which of these do contemporary practitioners actually sustain, and does the distinction matter for outcome?

Madhvācārya glosses 'divine' from a Bṛhadāraṇyaka passage about the omnipresent Puruṣa; Śaṅkara glosses the same word from a different śruti about the solar Person — how does the choice of śruti citation control the doctrinal conclusion without changing the Sanskrit text?

Vallabha reads nān'ya-gāminā as 'not moving even toward the Akṣara' — what psychological practice does that require beyond what ordinary non-distraction requires, and what does it imply about intermediate states of liberation?

All six schools agree that the mind must be 'not going elsewhere' — but they disagree on whether this is achieved by suppression, habituation, love, or samādhi's natural exhaustion of alternatives. Which mechanism is falsifiable?

Madhusūdana claims the samādhi-mind stops wandering 'even without deliberate suppression' (nirodhaprayatnaṃ vinā) — is this a higher stage than Chapter Six's abhyāsa that does require effort, or the same stage described from inside rather than outside?

The verse is addressed to Arjuna as 'Pārtha' — son of Pṛthā/Kuntī, not as Arjuna the warrior. Four of six commentators note this address; what is the significance of the maternal-lineage invocation at this point in the instruction?

advaita.application: Morning sitting: choose one object of inquiry — 'Who is aware right now?' — and return to it every time another thought arises, not by force but by recognition. The return itself, repeated without gap for whatever duration is available, is abhyāsa. Do not evaluate the quality of the sitting; evaluate only whether you returned. Over weeks, the mind that once required effort to return begins returning on its own — this is Śaṅkara's 'uninterrupted same-genus cognition' stabilizing as a disposition rather than a practice.

viśiṣṭādvaita.application: Maintain two registers simultaneously: informal rehearsal (abhyāsa) — brief internal acknowledgments of Bhagavān throughout ordinary activity, at transitions, before meals, at the start of each task — and formal scheduled upāsanā (yoga) at a fixed time each day, however short. Rāmānuja's point is that the formal session deepens the informal thread, and the informal thread keeps the formal session from becoming ritual-only. Missing one formal session is not failure; losing the informal thread is the actual loss.

dvaita.application: Treat every repeated action as an address to a distinct, living, fully present Hari who is not diminished by the repetition. The Madhva reading insists that Hari is not a concept to be internalized but a Person who is genuinely 'other' — sovereign, playful, real. In daily terms: when you return to a name, a form, a mantra in meditation, do so as you would return to a real conversation, not a self-generated internal state. The ontological gap is the practice; the practice is not aimed at closing it.

śuddhādvaita.application: The Vallabha reading demands that nothing — not even a noble spiritual object like the Imperishable — be allowed to become a stopping-point. In practice: when a meditation becomes comfortable, when a framework feels complete, when a spiritual identity feels settled — that is precisely the moment to move. Apply this at work: do not let a successful method become a fixed identity. Kṛṣṇa as the cosmogonic 'more subtle than the subtlest' means the target is always one register more refined than the last stable state.

bhakti.application: Śrīdhara's 'inner support' framing suggests a layered architecture: external practice (ritual, recitation, schedule) provides the structure, but the actual mechanism is the unbroken interior flow of same-genus awareness. Practically: if you find your outer practice mechanical, the prescription is not more outer practice but investigation of whether the interior flow has broken. Re-establish the interior thread first; the outer practice will re-animate. This applies directly to any sustained skill-building — the inner engagement (sajātīya-pratyaya-pravāha) is the load-bearing element.

advaita-bhakti.application: Madhusūdana's key practical contribution is 'nirodhaprayatnaṃ vinā' — the wandering stops without a suppression effort when the samādhi-quality matures. This is actionable feedback for any meditator: if you are still fighting distractions in meditation after years, the issue is not insufficient effort but insufficient samādhi-ripeness. The prescription is not more suppression but deeper immersion in the object itself — devotion as the accelerant of absorption, not as an alternative to it. Bhakti and jñāna converge in the same attentional mechanism.
